items.title: What to know when naming medicines in Brazil
items.description: Dual scrutiny by the Brazilian Patent and Trademark Office and Health Regulatory Agency demands careful coordination to avoid rejected brands or delayed products.

items.title: All UPC judges and their current capacities
items.description: The roster of judges at the UPC is steadily growing. While there were 124 legally and technically qualified judges in mid-2025, by the end of the year that number had already risen to 129. By March 2026, the number of judges had increased by another five as Thomas Adocker, Goda...

items.title: Sequence identities and functional definitions - where is the limit? (T 0137/24)
items.description: Biotechnology patents often rely on defining protein and nucleic acid features by their sequence identity. However, it is often not necessary to define the exact sequence, but instead to define the sequence according to a limit of sequence identity, e.g. a sequence having a 90%...

items.title: Trademarks are a “missed opportunity” in mining, expert says
items.description: Mining companies have historically underused trademarks, but brands must look beyond patents to keep up with an evolving industry, says Canadian IP partner.

items.title: EUIPO Report on IP-Backed Finance Makes Policy Recommendations to Unlock Up To €580 Billion in Innovation Financing
items.description: Today, the European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) published a study exploring challenges faced by EU small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in obtaining financing by offering intellectual property (IP) as collateral. Set against the backdrop of the EU’s recently...

items.title: [Guest post] Oh my influencer! Italian rules on influencer marketing

items.title: Stop Telling Yourself China’s Patent Boom Doesn’t Matter
items.description: I have been to China several times over the past decade. Each time, I came back with the same reaction: too many people in the United States are still badly underestimating what is happening there. I do not say that as a political statement. I say it as a practical one. There is...

items.title: From Delhi to Bombay, Music Licensing Goes Off the Beat
items.description: A lot has been happening with the repertoire of sound recordings that Phonographic Performance Limited (PPL) claims to own and manage. It is being used in saree showrooms, a group of 94 restaurants, high profile pubs and bars, restaurants and more. Against all these places, PPL...

items.title: Chinese court reinforces no copyright protection for AI-generated toy with no originality
items.description: The Yangzhou Intermediate People’s Court advises AI creators to preserve evidence of the creation process to prove intellectual input and personalised choices.

items.title: IPWatchdog Announces Leadership Promotions to Support Continued Growth and Strategic Expansion
items.description: IPWatchdog is happy to announce several leadership promotions to support its continued growth and strategic expansion. Renée C. Quinn has been named President, Katarzyna Kryca has been promoted to Senior Vice President, and Morgan Connell has been promoted to Director of...

items.title: Important update on Australia's flip-flopping "best method" requirement for divisional patent applications
items.description: Not only does Australia retain an archaic "best method" requirement for patents (IPKat), but the Full Court has confirmed that for divisional applications the best method is to be assessed at the time of filing the divisional application (not the parent application). The ongoing...

items.title: Europe falls behind in IP-backed financing, EUIPO report finds
items.description: A new EUIPO report highlights that only 13% of EU firms owning IP rights have tried to secure financing from these assets.

items.title: AI is Raising the Stakes on Intellectual Capital and IP Rights
items.description: For decades, management scholars and practitioners have grappled with what I call the "knowledge problem" in organizations—the stubborn difficulty of codifying and transferring expertise that resides in individual employees' heads and habits. The most valuable organizational...

items.title: On the way to a global FRAND rate: Nokia drops claims against Warner and Paramount
items.description: According to the UPC register, the proceedings concerning EP 4 250 732, over which Nokia had sued Warner Bros. and Paramount in autumn 2025, were withdrawn before Easter (case IDs: UPC_CFI_975/2025 and UPC_CFI_739/2026 and UPC_CFI_1390/2025). In response to an enquiry by JUVE...

items.title: Chinese courts are exploring more flexible time frames for enforcement of IP cases
items.description: Chinese courts and the Supreme People’s Court are experimenting with more flexible enforcement timelines in patent infringement cases where the validity of the underlying patent is in serious doubt. By extending the deadline for enforcing damages awards – sometimes by up to a...

items.title: Cox v Sony: Infringers gonna infringe – unless service providers encourage them
items.description: On 25 March, the US Supreme Court ruled that Cox Communications, Inc. is not contributorily liable for its users’ acts of copyright infringement against Sony Music Entertainment. Although two judges diverged from the majority in their reasoning, all agreed that Cox does not...

items.title: PTAB Inventory Drops Below 2,000 for the First Time in 20 Years
items.description: PTAB inventory drops below 2,000 for the first time as appeal pendency falls to 9 months and IPR institutions decline 43% under Dir. Squires. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.description: A March 27, 2026, petition filed by Security First Innovations (SFI) does more than challenge a single reexamination proceeding—it shines a spotlight on a structural vulnerability in how post-grant review is functioning in practice. At its core, the filing argues that the U.S....

items.title: Mumumelon dupe activism; QVC Group's scalable IP; 2026 WTR Awards shortlist; and much more
items.description: Your Sunday recap of the week’s biggest developments in trademark and brand protection.

items.description: Brand Action's London fundraiser arrives at a milestone moment – and there are still tickets available.

items.title: Mumumelon: the campaign using dupes as a “vehicle” for climate activism
items.description: Climate activist group Action Speaks Louder argues Mumumelon campaign does not “directly copy” Lululemon clothing.

items.title: Win without gain: Microsoft prevails in cost dispute with Suinno
items.description: Finnish company Suinno is the owner of EP 2 671 173 covering a “method and means for browsing by walking”. It relates to people browsing the internet by walking to discover search results as they come into close proximity. The Helsinki-based company accuses Microsoft of...

items.title: Friday Fantasies
items.description: While many have enjoyed a shorter work week, the IPKat has been busy keeping an eye out for events and opportunities that might interest our dear readers. Here's the latest updates.Forthcoming EventsImage by Gundula Vogel via PixabayThe next edition of the People, Plants and the...

items.title: Marked for Trouble: Settlement Licenses and the § 287 Trap for NPEs
items.description: Federal Circuit panel hammers NPE counsel on whether settlement licenses trigger the § 287 marking requirement for pre-suit patent damages. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: LEGO Minifigure win; Yuga Labs settlement; Ozempic copycat ruling – news digest
items.description: Six bite-sized IP updates from the week, also featuring Apollonia's settlement with the Prince estate, Jo Malone’s response to her name dispute with Estée Lauder, and Amazon's Counterfeit Crimes Unit launch in India.

items.title: Trump signals brand strategy shift with first TTAB cancellation actions
items.description: The principal trademark holding company for President Donald Trump is seeking cancellation of third-party registrations before the TTAB for what appears to be the first time.

items.title: WIPO is Seeking a Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) External Consultant
items.description: WIPO is seeking a Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) External Consultant. WIPO aims to significantly expand the use of the PCT system compared to the Paris Convention in the United States of America. To achieve this, WIPO will engage an external contractor for a time-bound...

items.title: Professors Push Back in Review of PTAB Rehearing Decision on ODP
items.description: Amicus briefs have now been posted to the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) site in Ex Parte Baurin, a 2025 rehearing decision of the Patent Trial and Appeal Board (PTAB) with respect to obviousness-type double patenting (ODP) that is being reviewed by an Appeals Review...

items.title: The Dark Matter of Patent Law: Nearly 25% of Office Actions Now Cite Secret Prior Art
items.description: Empirical study of 233M citations shows secret prior art is growing, but the legal measure overstates the practical problem by about a quarter. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: Your AI Is Saving You Time: So What?
items.description: I have spent most of my professional career talking to patent practitioners about AI. For years, the conversation was about whether AI could be trusted, whether it was ready, and whether it would actually change how patent work gets done. I have watched the profession move from...

items.title: New patent owner Silimed lands PI against Polytech at Munich court
items.description: Silimed has achieved its first success in the infringement dispute over breast implant technology. After the Brazilian manufacturer secured the rights to a patent for the manufacture of breast implants in a complex entitlement action, Silimed is now taking measures against its...

items.title: The Symmetry Problem: Printed Matter, Nexus, and the Federal Circuit’s One-Way Ratchet
items.description: Federal Circuit hears EagleView v. Nearmap on whether printed matter doctrine can be used to defeat secondary considerations nexus showing. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: [GuestPost] Keeping enemies closer: C&J Clark v TBC and trade mark coexistence agreements
items.description: The IPKat is grateful to Katfriend Thomas Hood (Gatehouse Chambers) for the following contribution on the recent UK decision in C & J Clark International ltd v Trek Bicycle Corporation & Anor [2026] EWHC 659 (Ch). Over to Tom:In the world of trade marks, businesses can often end...

items.title: X Marks the … Stick Figure? Federal Circuit Says One DuPont Factor Can Outweigh All Others
items.description: Federal Circuit affirms no likelihood of confusion between cigar maker's X marks and vape company's stick figure design, holding mark dissimilarity alone can be dispositive. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: Dyson wins another PI in hair-styler fight against Dreame
items.description: Once again, the Hamburg local division under presiding judge Sabine Klepsch has prohibited Chinese manufacturer Dreame from distributing certain hair styling devices in the UPC territory and Spain by means of a preliminary injunction. A PI issued in August last year covered...

items.title: (Part II) Right Without Duty: Academic Copyright, the Publisher’s Juridical Fiction, and the Case for an Ex Ante Reading of Section 52
items.description: Continuing the discussion on the Scihub litigation, in Part II of their post, Rishabh Upadhyay and Pragati Upadhyay turn to the August 2025 order as a vantage point to examine what the litigation has, so far, failed to do. They argue that the procedural exclusion of intervenors...

items.title: (Part I) Right Without Duty: Academic Copyright, the Publisher’s Juridical Fiction, and the Case for an Ex Ante Reading of Section 52
items.description: The Sci-Hub litigation has dragged on for years without confronting the core question it presents: what is the purpose of copyright in academic works? As the Court circles procedural issues, Rishabh Upadhyay and Pragati Upadhyay argue that a deeper structural failure remains...

items.title: Never Too Late: If you missed the IPKat last week!
items.description: Combing through legal archives and chocolate eggs is learned, but exhausting. If you, much like the Kats, spent the past few days taking a well-deserved Easter nap to sleep off the chocolate, you will be pleased to know it was a relatively quiet week on the blog too. Here is a...

items.title: Moving Target: When Amended Claims Outrun Your Standing Declaration
items.description: Federal Circuit dismisses PGR appeal in ironSource v. Digital Turbine, holding petitioner must link injury in fact to substitute claims, not just originals. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: Lundbeck and Biogaran fight in Milan and Paris over drug for alcohol dependence
items.description: Nalmefene is an opioid antagonist used in the EU to reduce alcohol dependence. The active ingredient works by antagonising those receptors in the brain responsible for producing the body’s feel-good chemical, dopamine. This prevents the alcohol-induced release of this chemical,...

items.title: After Crocs, After Carlsberg: Does Harpic v. Spic Finally Clear the Air?
items.description: Can trademark law be used to reclaim what design law has deliberately released into the public domain? Khushi Krishania writes on the Calcutta High Court’s recent Division Bench decision in the Harpic v. Spic dispute, explaining how it revisits this uneasy intersection, but...

items.title: How the Federal Circuit Is Rebuilding Inventorship Law After the AIA
items.description: The AIA repealed § 102(f) but inventorship invalidity endures. Tracing the defense from 1790 through Fortress Iron and AI. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: SpicyIP Weekly Review (March 30 – April 5)
items.description: Beginning April with a rundown of the major IP developments in 2025 on SpicyIP TV! Post on two recent Delhi High Court decisions in Geron and Hirotsu clarifying the boundaries of diagnostic methods exclusion. And another post on the UK Supreme Court’s decision in Emotional...

items.title: SpicyIP Bells & Whistles: IP Events and Opportunities (06.04.2026)
items.description: Welcome back to another week of Bells & Whistles. As always, we’ve rounded up a mix of developments, opportunities, and thoughtful reads from across the IP world along with a Bell of the Week that’s well worth revisiting. Bell of the Week: Margaret Boden Some bells do not just...

items.title: Missing Coinventors and the Death of a Patent
items.description: Federal Circuit holds patents invalid when missing coinventor cannot be found for § 256 correction. A case of first impression with AI implications. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: Most Common First Names of inventors on US Patents
items.description: Gif showing most common US patent inventor names 1910-2026. We know that in the US women have a lower rate of patenting. In addition though, parents are much more conservative in terms of male names. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: HGF boosts London chemistry and pharma practice with D Young partner
items.description: Garreth Duncan officially joined HGF’s London office on 1 April 2026, bringing with him considerable expertise across a broad range of chemical technologies. His practice has a strong focus on the pharmaceutical sector, where he specialises in protecting new chemical entities,...

items.title: Next US firm enters London patent market after CMS hire
items.description: It is not yet decided when Caitlin Heard will move to Perkins Coie as partner. Managing IP first reported the move. Heard currently serves as co-head of the international IP practice group at CMS Cameron McKenna Nabarro Olswang. She is qualified as a solicitor advocate and also...

items.title: Diagnosing the Diagnostic Exclusion: Have Geron and Hirotsu Resolved the Delhi High Court’s Troubles?
items.description: Section 3(i) of the Patents Act has long left the boundaries of diagnostic method exclusions uncertain, despite repeated judicial engagement. Two recent Delhi High Court decisions now offer the clearest articulation yet, bringing much-needed coherence to this evolving area of...

items.title: Words That Stick: Prosecution Disclaimer Survives the Examiner’s Rejection
items.description: Federal Circuit holds in Puradigm v. DBG that a prosecution disclaimer stands even when the examiner rejected the disclaiming argument, extending the disclaimer to logical variants of disclaimed prior art. Continue reading this post on Patently-O.

items.title: De Koffiejongens averts PI filed by Ox Barrier in the Netherlands
items.description: The case is reminiscent of the patent dispute over coffee capsules between manufacturers Nespresso, Krups, De’Longhi and Ethical Coffee Company (ECC), which broke out between 2016 and 2018 in Germany and Switzerland, as well as the more recent dispute between Koninklijke Douwe...

items.title: The UKSC’s “Any Hardware” Shortcut: Why Emotional Perception AI Raises More Questions Than It Answers — And Why India’s Framework Does Better
items.description: The UK Supreme Court’s decision in Emotional Perception AI Limited v Comptroller General of Patents marks a doctrinal shift in how subject-matter eligibility questions regarding computer programmes are evaluated. Irrespective of its impact, Pragati argues that the judgment is...

items.title: WIPO Launches New Award for Cities of Innovation
items.description: The World Intellectual Property Organization (WIPO) today launched the “WIPO City of Innovation” award program, a new initiative that recognizes cities worldwide demonstrating exceptional commitment to fostering innovation and creativity among their residents.

items.title: WIPO Launches Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure Interchange
items.description: WIPO today launched the Artificial Intelligence Infrastructure Interchange (AIII), a new initiative for expert dialogue on IP and AI issues that will focus on technical and operational issues and complement ongoing discussions at other WIPO forums.

items.title: Albania is First to Join WIPO’s Riyadh Design Law Treaty
items.description: Albania today became the first member state to join WIPO’s Riyadh Design Law Treaty (RDLT), depositing its instrument of ratification with Director General Daren Tang and marking a major milestone in bringing into force the new international framework for design protection.

items.title: International Patent Applications Rose in 2025, with Digital Communication and Semiconductor Technologies Showing Strong Growth
items.description: International patent applications filed through WIPO's Patent Cooperation Treaty (PCT) rose 0.7% in 2025 to reach 275,900 filings worldwide, marking a second consecutive year of growth. The increase was partly due to robust activity in ICT related fields, with digital...

items.title: World Intellectual Property Report: Pace of Innovation Diffusion Reaches Historical Heights, But Access, Usage and Capability Gaps Remain
items.description: New technologies are spreading across borders at unprecedented pace and gaps are narrowing in how intensively countries are using the innovations, the latest World Intellectual Property Report (WIPR) 2026, Technology on the Move finds.
